question:
Two bodies of masses m1=5 kg and m2=3 kg are connected by a light string going over a smooth light pulley on a smooth inclined plane as shown in the figure. The system is at rest. The force exerted by the inclined plane on the body of mass m1 will be : [Take g=10 m s-2]

​​​​​​​
solution:
The forces acting on two masses are shown below.

Here, N is normal to the incline and tension T along the string up the incline. As the string and the pulley are all light and smooth, the tension in the string is uniform everywhere.

​​​​​​​

For equilibrium condition, forces should add to zero.

Thus, equating, m2g=m1gsinθ

⇒sinθ=m2m1=35 and cosθ=45

Normal force on m1 is N=m1gcosθ=5gcosθ

=5×10×45=40 N

subject: Physics
q: A block <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> to slide down a frictionless incline of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>, kept inside a lift going up with uniform velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. If the incline is changed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, the time taken by the block, to slide down the incline, will be approximately:
question: A block <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> to slide down a frictionless incline of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>, kept inside a lift going up with uniform velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. If the incline is changed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, the time taken by the block, to slide down the incline, will be approximately:
solution:
As per the information given in the question the lift is moving upward with uniform velocity. Therefore, the frame of reference attached to the lift is an inertial frame of reference. The acceleration of the block down the incline will be,

a=gsinθ

Using equation of motion for constant acceleration,

s=ut+12at2

⇒l=12gsin30°22       ...1

When the inclination is changed to 45°,

l=12gsin45°t2       ...2

Equating the above equations,

12gsin30°22=12gsin45°t2 

⇒124=12t2⇒t=22=1.68 s

subject: Physics
q: A bullet of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass is fired from a gun of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the bullet moves with the muzzle speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mn>1</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> the impulse imparted to the gun and velocity of recoil of gun are
question: A bullet of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass is fired from a gun of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the bullet moves with the muzzle speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mn>1</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> the impulse imparted to the gun and velocity of recoil of gun are
solution:
By momentum conservation,
4×10-3(50-v)-4v=0
v=4×10-3×504+4×10-3≈0.05 m s-1
Impulse  J=mv=4×.05=0.2 kg m s-1

q: As shown in the figure a block of mass 10 kg lying on a horizontal surface is pulled by a force F acting at an angle 30°, with horizontal. For μs=0.25, the block will just start to move for the value of F: [Given g = 10 m·s–2]
question: As shown in the figure a block of mass 10 kg lying on a horizontal surface is pulled by a force F acting at an angle 30°, with horizontal. For μs=0.25, the block will just start to move for the value of F: [Given g = 10 m·s–2]
solution:
The free body diagram for the given scenario is shown below-

From the equilibrium of the vertical component of forces, it can be written that

N=mg-F sin 30°=mg-F2=100-F2=200-F2........................(1)

From the equilibrium of horizontal component of forces, it can be written that,

F cos 30°= μN......................(2)

Substitute the expression for the normal reaction force from equation (1) into equation (2) and solve to calculate the value of the applied force.

3F2=0.25×200-F243F=200-FF=20043+1≈25.2

subject: Physics
q: Sand is being dropped on a conveyer belt at the rate of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ per second. The force necessary to keep the belt moving with a constant speed of $3 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ will be
question: Sand is being dropped on a conveyer belt at the rate of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ per second. The force necessary to keep the belt moving with a constant speed of $3 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ will be
solution:
Here, rate of fall of sand on conveyer belt,
$$
\frac{d m}{d t}=2 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}
$$
Conveyer belt speed $v=3 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ Force $F=$ ?
$$
F=v \frac{d m}{d t}=3 \times 2=6 \mathrm{~N}
$$

question: Given in the figure are two blocks A and B of weight 20 N and 100 N, respectively. These are being pressed against a wall by a force F and kept in equilibrium as shown. If the coefficient of friction between the blocks is 0.1 and between block B and the wall is 0.15, the frictional force applied by the wall on block B is:
solution: For complete state equilibrium of the system. The state friction on the block B by the wall will balance the total weight 120 N of the system.

subject: Physics
q: A mass of $1\text{ kg}$ is kept on an inclined plane with $30°$ inclination with respect to horizontal plane and it is at rest initially. Then the whole assembly is moved up with constant velocity of $4\text{ m/s}$. The work done by the frictional force in time $2\text{ s}$ is _______ J. (Take $g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
question: A mass of $1\text{ kg}$ is kept on an inclined plane with $30°$ inclination with respect to horizontal plane and it is at rest initially. Then the whole assembly is moved up with constant velocity of $4\text{ m/s}$. The work done by the frictional force in time $2\text{ s}$ is _______ J. (Take $g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
solution:
Since the assembly moves with a constant velocity, the acceleration of the mass is zero. The mass is in equilibrium relative to the incline.

The frictional force $f$ acting on the mass balances the component of gravity along the inclined plane. Therefore, the magnitude of the frictional force is:

$f = mg \sin 30^\circ$

Substituting the given values ($m = 1\text{ kg}$, $g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$):

$f = 1 \times 10 \times \dfrac{1}{2} = 5\text{ N}$

The direction of the frictional force is upwards along the incline. Since the incline makes an angle of $30^\circ$ with the horizontal, the frictional force makes an angle of $90^\circ - 30^\circ = 60^\circ$ with the vertical upward direction.

The displacement $s$ of the assembly in time $t = 2\text{ s}$ is vertically upwards:

$s = vt = 4 \times 2 = 8\text{ m}$

The work done by the frictional force is:

$W = f s \cos 60^\circ$

$W = 5 \times 8 \times \dfrac{1}{2} = 20\text{ J}$

Answer: $20$

The average translational kinetic energy of N2 gas molecules at _________C∘ becomes equal to the K.E. of an electron accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 0.1 volt.

(Given kB=1.38×10-23 J K-1 (Fill the nearest integer).
question:

(Given kB=1.38×10-23 J K-1 (Fill the nearest integer).
solution:
Given

Translation K.E. of N2=K.E of electron

32kT=eV

32×1.38×10-23 T=1.6×10-19×0.1  ⇒T=773 K

T=773-273=500°C

subject: Physics
q: A block of weight $W$ rests on a horizontal floor with coefficient of static friction $\mu$. It is desired to make the block move by applying minimum amount of force. The angle $\theta$ from the horizontal at which the force should be applied and magnitude of the force $F$ are respectively.
question: A block of weight $W$ rests on a horizontal floor with coefficient of static friction $\mu$. It is desired to make the block move by applying minimum amount of force. The angle $\theta$ from the horizontal at which the force should be applied and magnitude of the force $F$ are respectively.
solution:
Let the force $F$ is applied at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.
For horizontal equilibrium, $F \cos \theta=\mu R$ For vertical equilibrium, $R+F \sin \theta=\mathrm{mg}$ or, $R=m g-F \sin \theta$ Substituting this value of $R$ in eq. (i), we get $$ \begin{aligned} & F \cos \theta=\mu(\mathrm{mg}-F \sin \theta) \\ & =\mu \mathrm{mg}-\mu F \sin \theta \\ & \text { or, } F(\cos \theta+\mu \sin \theta)=\mu \mathrm{mg} \\ & \text { or, } \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu \mathrm{mg}}{\cos \theta+\mu \sin \theta} \end{aligned} $$ For $F$ to be minimum, the denominator $(\cos \theta+\mu \sin \theta)$ should be maximum. $$ \therefore \frac{d}{d \theta}(\cos \theta+\mu \sin \theta)=0 $$ or, $-\sin \theta+\mu \cos \theta=0$ or, $\tan \theta=\mu$ or, $\theta=\tan ^{-1}(\mu)$ Then, $\sin \theta=\frac{\mu}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}}$ and $$ \cos \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}} $$ Hence, $F_{\text {min }}$ $$ =\frac{\mu w}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}}+\frac{\mu^2}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}}}=\frac{\mu w}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}} $$

Time taken to slide along a smooth surface is t1.
S=12gsin⁡45ot12
t1=22sg
Time taken to slide along a rough surface is t2.
S=12gsin⁡45o-μgsin⁡45o t22
t2=22sg1-μ
t2=nt1
22sg1-μ=n2×22sg⇒1-μ=1n2⇒μ=1-1n2

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass $m$ falls from rest through a resistive medium having resistive force, $F=-k v$, where $v$ is the velocity of the particle and $k$ is a constant. Which of the following graphs represents velocity ($v$) versus time ($t$)?
question: A particle of mass $m$ falls from rest through a resistive medium having resistive force, $F=-k v$, where $v$ is the velocity of the particle and $k$ is a constant. Which of the following graphs represents velocity ($v$) versus time ($t$)?
solution:
The particle experiences downward gravitational force (mg) and upward resistive force (-kv).
The equation of motion is $m\frac{dv}{dt} = mg - kv$.
Initially at $t=0$, $v=0$ gives maximum acceleration $a_0 = g$.
As velocity increases, the resistive force increases, reducing the net acceleration.
Eventually the particle reaches terminal velocity $v_t = \frac{mg}{k}$ when acceleration becomes zero.
The velocity-time graph shows an increasing curve starting from origin with decreasing slope, asymptotically approaching the terminal velocity.
This behavior matches option (1), which displays a curve starting at (0,0) with steep initial slope that gradually flattens.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : An electric fan continues to rotate for some time after the current is switched off.

Reason R: Fan continues to rotate due to inertia of motion.

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : An electric fan continues to rotate for some time after the current is switched off.

Reason R: Fan continues to rotate due to inertia of motion.

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: A is correct but R is not correct
A is not correct but R is correct
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
When a fan is rotating, it acquires some kinetic energy. When the switch is off, because of its motion, it continues rotating for some time until it stops because of the air friction. So it is due to inertia of motion that fan continues to rotate. 

Hence, both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A.

subject: Physics
q: The boxes of masses 2 kg and 8 kg are connected by a massless string passing over smooth pulleys. Calculate the time taken by box of mass 8 kg to strike the ground starting from rest.  g=10 m s-2
question: The boxes of masses 2 kg and 8 kg are connected by a massless string passing over smooth pulleys. Calculate the time taken by box of mass 8 kg to strike the ground starting from rest.  g=10 m s-2
solution: \(\left(\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{~g}-2 \mathrm{~T}\right)=\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{a}-(1)\) \(\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{~g}=\mathrm{m}_2(2 \mathrm{a})\) \(2 \mathrm{~T}-2 \mathrm{~m}_2 \mathrm{~g}=4 \mathrm{~m}_2 \mathrm{a}-(2)\) \(\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{~g}-2 \mathrm{~m}_2 \mathrm{~g}=\left(\mathrm{m}_1+4 \mathrm{~m}_2\right) \mathrm{a}\) \(\mathrm{a}=\frac{(8-4) \mathrm{g}}{(8+8)}=\frac{4}{16} \mathrm{~g}=\frac{\mathrm{g}}{4}\) \(\mathrm{a}=\frac{10}{4} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\) \(\mathrm{~S}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{at}^2\) \(\frac{0.2 \times 2 \times 4}{10}=\mathrm{t}^2\) \(\mathrm{t}=0.4 \mathrm{sec}\)

q: A particle of mass $0.3 \mathrm{~kg}$ is subjected to a force $F=-k x$ with $k=15 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. What will be its initial acceleration if it is released from a point $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ away from the origin?
question: A particle of mass $0.3 \mathrm{~kg}$ is subjected to a force $F=-k x$ with $k=15 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. What will be its initial acceleration if it is released from a point $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ away from the origin?
solution: $a=\frac{k x}{m}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in such a way that at any instant the normal and tangential components of its acceleration are equal. If its speed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the time taken to complete the first revolution will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
question: A particle is moving in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in such a way that at any instant the normal and tangential components of its acceleration are equal. If its speed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the time taken to complete the first revolution will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
solution:
Given:

a→c=a→t

⇒v2r=dvdt

⇒∫4vdvv2=∫0tdtr

⇒-1v4v=tr

⇒-1v+14=2t

⇒v=41-8t=dsdt

⇒4∫0tdt1-8t=∫0sds

r=0.5 m, distance covered s=2πr=π

⇒4×[ln(1-8t)]0t-8=π

⇒ℓn(1-8t)=-2π

⇒1-8t=e-2π

⇒t=1-e-2π18 s

So, α=8.

subject: Physics
q: A flexible chain of mass $m$ hangs between two fixed points at the same level. The inclination of the chain with the horizontal at the two points of support is $30^{\circ}$. Considering the equilibrium of each half of the chain, the tension of the chain at the lowest point is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: A flexible chain of mass $m$ hangs between two fixed points at the same level. The inclination of the chain with the horizontal at the two points of support is $30^{\circ}$. Considering the equilibrium of each half of the chain, the tension of the chain at the lowest point is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
A chain of mass $m$ hangs between two fixed points at the same level with inclination $30°$ at support points.
Consider equilibrium of one half of the chain (mass $m/2$):
Horizontal equilibrium: $T_0 = T_1\cos(30°) = T_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
Vertical equilibrium: $T_1\sin(30°) = \frac{m}{2}g$
From vertical equation: $T_1 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{mg}{2}$, giving $T_1 = mg$
Substituting into horizontal equation: $T_0 = mg \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}mg$

subject: Physics
q: Two masses $m_1=5 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=4.8 \mathrm{~kg}$ tied to a string are hanging over a light frictionless pulley. What is the acceleration of the masses when lift free to move $\left(\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
question: Two masses $m_1=5 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=4.8 \mathrm{~kg}$ tied to a string are hanging over a light frictionless pulley. What is the acceleration of the masses when lift free to move $\left(\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
solution: $\mathrm{a}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}_1-\mathrm{m}_2}{\mathrm{~m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2}\right) \mathrm{g}=0.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass $m$ is suspended by two strings making angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ with the horizontal ceiling with tensions $T_1$ and $T_2$ simultaneously. $T_1$ and $T_2$ are related by $T_1=\sqrt{3} T_2$. the angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are
question: A body of mass $m$ is suspended by two strings making angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ with the horizontal ceiling with tensions $T_1$ and $T_2$ simultaneously. $T_1$ and $T_2$ are related by $T_1=\sqrt{3} T_2$. the angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}_1 \sin \theta_1+\mathrm{T}_2 \sin \theta_2=\mathrm{mg} \& \mathrm{~T}_1=\sqrt{3} \mathrm{~T}_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~T}_2\left[\sqrt{3} \sin \theta_1+\sin \theta_2\right]=\mathrm{mg} \\ & \text { for } \theta_1=60^{\circ} \& \theta_2=30^{\circ} \\ & \mathrm{T}_2=\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{2}\end{aligned}$

question: A block of mass ' $\mathrm{m}$ ' is connected to another block of mass ' $\mathrm{M}$ ' by a spring (massless) of spring constant ' $\mathrm{k}$ '. The blocks are kept on a smooth horizontal plane. Initially the blocks are at rest and the spring is unstretched. Then a constant force '$\mathrm{F}$' starts acting on the block of mass '$\mathrm{M}$' to pull it. Find the force on the block of mass '$\mathrm{m}$'
solution: $\mathrm{Kx}=\mathrm{ma}=\frac{\mathrm{mF}}{\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{M}}$

subject: Physics

solution:

subject: Physics
q: A given object takes $n$ times more time to slide down a $45^{\circ}$ rough inclined plane as it takes to slide down a perfectly smooth $45^{\circ}$ incline. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the object and the incline is :
question: A given object takes $n$ times more time to slide down a $45^{\circ}$ rough inclined plane as it takes to slide down a perfectly smooth $45^{\circ}$ incline. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the object and the incline is :
solution:
The coefficients of kinetic friction between the object and the incline
$\mu=\tan \theta\left(1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \mu=1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2} \quad\left(\because \theta=45^{\circ}\right)$

subject: Physics
q: A light unstretchable string passing over a smooth light pulley connects two blocks of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$. If the acceleration of the system is $\frac{g}{8}$, then the ratio of the masses $\frac{m_2}{m_1}$ is :
question: A light unstretchable string passing over a smooth light pulley connects two blocks of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$. If the acceleration of the system is $\frac{g}{8}$, then the ratio of the masses $\frac{m_2}{m_1}$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & a_{\text {sys }}=\left(\frac{m_2-m_1}{m_1+m_2}\right) g=\frac{g}{8} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{m_2}{m_1}=\frac{9}{7}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A block of mass 5 kg is (i) pushed in case A and (ii) pulled in case B, by a force F=20 N, making an angle of 30o with the horizontal, as shown in the figures. The coefficient of friction between the block and floor is μ=0.2. The difference between the accelerations of the block, in case B and case A will be:
g=10 m s-2
question:
A block of mass 5 kg is (i) pushed in case A and (ii) pulled in case B, by a force F=20 N, making an angle of 30o with the horizontal, as shown in the figures. The coefficient of friction between the block and floor is μ=0.2. The difference between the accelerations of the block, in case B and case A will be:
g=10 m s-2
solution:
For Block A:

N=mg+Fsin⁡30°
=5×10+20×12
=60 N
fr=0.260= 12 N
Fcos⁡30° –fr= ma
2032 -12=5×a
⇒aA=103-125 ……(i)
For Block B:

Fsin⁡30°+N=mg
N=5×10 –20×12
=40 N
fr=0.2×40=8 N
Fcos30° –fr=ma
2032-8=5×a
⇒aB=103-85
aB-aA=45=0.8 m/s2

subject: Physics
q: A spring balance is attached to the ceiling of a lift. A man hangs his bag on the spring and the spring reads 49 $\mathrm{N}$, when the lift is stationary. If the lift moves downward with an acceleration of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, the reading of the spring balance will be
question: A spring balance is attached to the ceiling of a lift. A man hangs his bag on the spring and the spring reads 49 $\mathrm{N}$, when the lift is stationary. If the lift moves downward with an acceleration of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, the reading of the spring balance will be
solution: Mass $=\frac{49}{9.8}=5 \mathrm{~kg}$. When lift is moving downward, apparent weight $=5(9.8-5)=5 \times 4.8=24 \mathrm{~N}$

subject: Physics
q:
A block of mass m slides on the wooden wedge, which in turn slides backward on the horizontal surface. The acceleration of the block with respect to the wedge is:
Given m=8 kg, M=16 kg
Assume all the surfaces shown in the figure to be frictionless.
question:
A block of mass m slides on the wooden wedge, which in turn slides backward on the horizontal surface. The acceleration of the block with respect to the wedge is:
Given m=8 kg, M=16 kg
Assume all the surfaces shown in the figure to be frictionless.
solution:
Ncos60°=Ma1=16a1
⇒N=32a1
⊥ to incline

N=8gcos30°-8a1sin30°⇒32a1=443g-4a1

⇒  a2=39g

Along incline

8gsin30°+8a1cos30°=ma2=8a2

a2=g×12+39g·32=2g3

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> starts sliding on a surface with an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The coefficient of friction between the surface and block is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>. The distance covered by the block before coming to rest is :[use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> starts sliding on a surface with an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The coefficient of friction between the surface and block is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>. The distance covered by the block before coming to rest is :[use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Given initial velocity u=9.8 m s-1.

Acceleration a=μg=0.5×9.8=4.9 m s-2

Using, v2=u2+2as, here, final velocity v=0.

So, 0=9.82+24.9s⇒s=9.8 m

q: A cricket player catches a ball of mass 120 g moving with 25 m s-1 speed. If the catching process is completed in 0.1 s then the magnitude of force exerted by the ball on the hand of player will be(in SI unit):
question: A cricket player catches a ball of mass 120 g moving with 25 m s-1 speed. If the catching process is completed in 0.1 s then the magnitude of force exerted by the ball on the hand of player will be(in SI unit):
solution:
Average force exerted will be, Fav=ΔpΔt

=0-0.12×250.1=30 N

subject: Physics
q:
A mass of 10 kg is suspended vertically by a rope of length 5 m from the roof. A force of 30 N is applied at the middle point of rope in horizontal direction. The angle made by upper half of the rope with vertical is α=tan-1x×10-1. The value of x is _____ .

(Given, g=10 m s-2)
question:
A mass of 10 kg is suspended vertically by a rope of length 5 m from the roof. A force of 30 N is applied at the middle point of rope in horizontal direction. The angle made by upper half of the rope with vertical is α=tan-1x×10-1. The value of x is _____ .

(Given, g=10 m s-2)
solution:
⇒Tsinθ=100 and Tcosθ=30

Therefore, tanθ=103

Then, tanα=3 10⇒α=tan-13×10-1

Hence, value of x=3.

subject: Physics
q: A heavy box of mass $50 \mathrm{~kg}$ is moving on a horizontal surface. If co-efficient of kinetic friction between the box and horizontal surface is 0.3 then force of kinetic friction is :
question: A heavy box of mass $50 \mathrm{~kg}$ is moving on a horizontal surface. If co-efficient of kinetic friction between the box and horizontal surface is 0.3 then force of kinetic friction is :
solution: $F_k=\mu_k N=0.3 \times 50 \times 9.8=147 N$
